does: This paper investigates adversarial vulnerabilities in speech-language models (SLMs) that integrate speech recognition with large language models. The authors design white-box and black-box attack algorithms capable of generating adversarial examples to jailbreak SLMs without human intervention, testing these on a curated dataset of harmful questions. They additionally propose countermeasures to defend against such attacks. The paper evaluates models trained on dialog data with speech instructions, achieving over 80% performance on both safety and helpfulness metrics in spoken question-answering tasks. Crucially, despite integrated safety guardrails, experiments reveal substantial vulnerabilities: achieving approximately 90% success rate on white-box jailbreaking attacks and 10% success on transfer attacks, demonstrating that current SLMs remain fragile against adversarial perturbations in the audio domain.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on adversarial robustness in multimodal AI systems, building on established attack methodologies from both text-based and audio domains. It connects to prior research on jailbreaking large language models and adversarial examples in speech recognition, while addressing a gap in understanding robustness specifically for integrated speech-language systems. The paper draws on adversarial attack frameworks previously developed for vision and NLP tasks, adapting them to the unique challenge of jointly manipulating audio inputs that must pass speech recognition before reaching language model safety filters. This work also engages with emerging concerns about multimodal system vulnerabilities and the tension between model capability and safety in instruction-following systems.
enables: This research establishes a foundation for developing more robust speech-language interfaces and points toward several future directions. It enables the creation of better defensive mechanisms tailored specifically to multimodal attack surfaces, where adversaries can exploit vulnerabilities at the speech recognition layer to circumvent downstream safety mechanisms. The findings motivate research into speech-aware safety training, adversarial robustness certification for audio inputs, and detection methods for adversarial audio. More broadly, this work supports the development of evaluation benchmarks for multimodal system safety and enables future work on understanding how perturbations at different modalities compound security risks. The identified vulnerabilities also motivate investigation into fundamental trade-offs between model capability, interpretability, and robustness in systems combining speech and language understanding.
